sentences.text: Did you hook me back up?
OK.
I think we were discussing my revisit to Fort Sill, Oklahoma where I went for what they call liaison pilot training.
And this was in early 1943.
One of the thing is that I'll never forget-- having been to Fort Sill before, and having been to Texas before-- I got very accustomed to their Country Western music, which we just call Texican music.

And-- and also, having been from Tucson, Arizona, which was not nearly as Country Western as Texas or Oklahoma, the song that used to drive us all nuts both in 1940, 1941, and still in 1943 was the 'Yellow Rose of Texas.'
'San Antonio Rose.' OK, 'San Antonio Rose.' This started in the-- in 1940.
And I'll just do a little.
Excuse me.
I lost my voice 50 years ago, but it-- it's [SINGING] Deep within my heart lies a melody, a song of old San Anton.
And that goes on and on about-- about San Antonio Rose.
I think that's all I can remember.
It's a real pretty song.
But you know, every radio station had that sucker on all the time.
So anyhow, I finished my flight training at Fort Sill, became-- got my wings as-- as an [?
L-pilot, ?] went back to Camp Adair.
The unit then moved-- the whole-- our division, the 70th division, moved to Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri in the summer-- well, it was the same year, 1943-- late-- later in '43.
Now, Fort Leonard Wood is a military-- a regular fort military post, been there for many, many years.
It's up in the Ozark mountains about 60 miles from St.
Louis.
A good fort, a good post.
We had-- we had nice barracks.
It was really very, very pleasant.
I was flying the liaison planes by then, which was for artillery spotting.
It was basically for the eyes of the big guns.
We would pick targets and find enemy-- that's what we were training now to do in Europe.

All this training in-- in process of our training, we were told by people who had come back from combat that the Germans would not shoot at these little airplanes because you had a battalion of field artillery that you could get on them in about two seconds.
So you could have 12 105 Howitzers on a target in about-- less than 10 seconds.
So they would never shoot at you.
That's what we were told.
Didn't turn out to be the case, but that's what we were told.
Still, pursuing my dream of becoming a cadet in the air corps, Army Air Corps, I began to realize that it wouldn't be too long until this unit was going to go into combat overseas.
Because some of the-- our sister units had left already and were in Europe, the ones that were trained, had train with us in Camp Adair.
This 104th division, the 96th division, and these fellows had already-- were already over there.
So an opportunity came for me to-- to go to the parachute school at Fort Benning, Georgia, which is at Columbus.
And this is an old military post, and not a-- and not a very nice place.
It was one of the-- one of the most difficult things, maybe with the possible exception of the Louisiana maneuvers, that I endured in the States in training.
The summer-- hot, miserable, tough like the-- the commandos.
Everybody was-- rank didn't mean-- I was a staff sergeant.
Rank didn't mean a thing.
Majors, colonels, lieutenants, sergeant-- didn't matter.
These-- these PE instructors just were the boss.
And they would make-- you never walked.
You ran every place you went for 8 to 12 hours a day, every day, seven days a week.
And they were training you to be tough.
And you are going to be a paratrooper, and that was the bottom line.
So I-- I went through that class, got my mandatory five jumps in.
I was asked if I would like to be an instructor because I was in very good physical condition.
I could run all day, 20, 30 miles a day.
And I weighed about a 160 pounds.
And my-- my F-- my youth with-- as a football player-- all that really had now began to pay off for me because I was really in good-- in good condition.

So I became a PE instructor with a promise of a promotion from the commanding officer of the-- of the Fort Benning, Georgia jump school, who was Colonel Bronko Nagurski who was a professional wrestler in the old days.
You may have heard of him.
He was a football player, All-American football player, and he was also a professional wrestler.
Huge-- Could you repeat his name?
Bronko Nagurski.
I met the man twice.
And he was awesome.
Nice, but he was awesome.
He was a colonel.
And so, anyhow, he was the one that gave me the opportunity to become an constructor, which meant, OK, if they do go overseas-- this was my thinking-- I won't have to go as long as I'm here.
But I had to make a jump a month, you know, to-- to keep my-- to get my jump pay, which is 50% of your base pay.
And I made 5 jumps, and I didn't like that.
I made a total of 17 jumps altogether.
It never was any fun for me.
I was-- I just didn't-- the idea of jumping out of an airplane at 5,000 or 6,000 feet just didn't appeal to me.
And I had seen a few guys that had died in the process.
And it wasn't a pretty sight.
But I did this for six weeks.
And while I was-- and I thought, boy I'm going to-- I've got a real cushy thing here.
I had a-- a little room off-base, which, by the time I got to be an instructor, I could leave and-- and go into Columbus and, you know, and stay in a room, as opposed to being in the barracks.
But I got my call from there to go to-- to flight training with the Army Air Corps.
So I went to Repo Depot in Texas, a replacement where they said, OK, here's 1,000 cadets.
What are you going to know?
You guys are going here.
You guys are going there.
And I went to Marana, Arizona for primary flight training, which I was already a pilot.
But you still had to go through the-- you still had to go through the Air Corps training.
So it didn't take me long.
I soloed in three hours in-- in the-- in the Air Corps primary school and was-- and went to advanced.
You know, I here, again, I had an advantage of already having been through some of this.
So it was real easy for me.
I was a natural.
And some of the guys that had been four year-- that had their four-year degrees-- engineering degree were instructors.
They had already been to Europe and had their mission-- had done 50 missions and come back as instructors.
So I had some of the best instructors at Marana, and then at Thunderbird too in-- in Phoenix, Litchfield.
These men taught us combat training.
And-- and we were flying BT-13s and AT-6s and P-47s.
Then when we got through with the-- with the-- became-- flight officer is what they called us at the time-- where we weren't an enlisted man or officer.
We were flight officers.
Kind of like a warrant officer, in between, and with a promise of a commission.
They send some of us to-- some of that graduating class, now, this is-- this is in the Army Air Corps-- to Long Island for advanced training, combat training.
I went to Louisiana.
And I don't remember where I was in Louisiana.
It was just-- it was-- I didn't-- I hated the idea of going back there.
But I went to Louisiana to a-- to an Army-- Army air base in Louisiana for tactic, combat tactical training-- and then went to Boston.
Am-- am I-- am I getting too confusing here?
Now, this is in the-- this is in the fall or late summer of 1944.
We sailed on the Queen Mary and went to-- and I don't know where we landed.
It seems like it was Liverpool, England, but I don't really remember.
The journey was-- because of the-- we were not in a convoy.
The ship zigzagged.
We went clear down-- from Boston down to Florida and went across the ocean like this.
Yeah, that was that-- that-- the Queen Mary.
I also went-- and now, that's another story.

I came back on another ship on-- for special training, was in the States for two months in 1943, let's see, '44-- and went back over on the Mariposa, which was another-- and it was a big cruiser, a big luxury liner, which was like the Queen Mary only smaller.
And--
I-- well, it was-- they-- there was no way they could transport large numbers of men with airplanes in those days.
They can do it now.
They didn't have any-- they did-- they-- they just couldn't do it.
It was logistically very difficult.
There was only two ways to do it.
One was to go the northern , route which was through from Maine to-- to Nova Scotia to Scotland and Greenland.
And the weather it was always terrible.
And there were the German fighters in that vicinity watching for that to try to happen.
And they had you had a big disadvantage if you're in a passenger plane.
So they didn't try to transport large numbers by air.
The other way was from-- from the-- from the coast down South to some islands-- [INAUDIBLE] right on the tip of my tongue.
And they do this now.
It's-- it's a-- it's a resort area now, and not Trinidad, but it's one of those Caribbean islands.
And you hop scotched, and they did this with airplanes that had four engines.
Navy ships?
Oh.
--well, they--
--didn't have any transports big enough.
They would take 10,000, 15,000 men across at a time in the-- on the Queen Mary or on the Mariposa.
Yeah and was-- and was assigned at-- at this point in time, I was a-- I was a replacement pilot, a utility replacement pilot, and went to a repo depot, they call them, a replacement depot.
I was assigned to the Ninth Air Force, to the 10th Fighter Group of the Ninth Air Force in England, at-- at Raydon, England.
Went through some more combat training and flew escort missions across the English Channel to France, into France, and Holland, and-- and Germany a little bit.
Escorting bombers.
And then, the P-51s came in.
The P-47s were much more shorter range.
The P-51s had twice as much range as the P-47s did.
So they took over the job pretty much of escorting the bombers.
They could go clear to Berlin and back without any trouble.
So my unit was then assigned to air-to-ground support.
And so, we were-- we did what was called targets of opportunity.
Or an assigned target from air to ground.
The ground would call us in and say we have-- we have a little-- a village, or some tanks, or some obstacle that we can't get through.
Come in and-- and tear it up.
And-- and so, we would take a squadron of P-47s.
Each had two 1,000 pound bombs and 3,500 rounds of 50 caliber ammunition.
And we were-- a lot of firepower.
And we would, say the infantry or the-- or whoever was on the ground that was stopped, having trouble, would give us targets.
They'd use smoke and whatever.
And we would go in there, and sometimes we didn't see anything except the smoke.
We would drop our bombs, and strafe, and raise hell, tear up the territory.
And-- and they would tell us, you know, we got it or we didn't.
If we didn't, we'd have to go back and reload and come back.
With sometime-- that was called a sortie-- and sometimes, and some-- on some days you could-- might do four or five sorties.
Because it was, you know, you were only 20, 30 miles from your-- from your base, which in this case was in France.
It was Luneville, France where we were based at that time.
We had left England, gone to France after D-day.
And they-- then they had scattered the Eighth and the Ninth Air Force, mostly the Ninth, all over in different places.
And this-- the Ninth Air Force was a medium-- we didn't have any heavy bombers, didn't have any B-17s.
The biggest airplanes we had were B-26s.
The twin engines and B-25s, twin engines, very fast, very, very powerful bombers.
And we flew escort missions with them too.
But I mean targets were-- were opportunity.
Trains, convoys, troops if you could find them.
We were-- we were train busters pretty much.
We-- when we killed a train, we got an engine painted on our fuselage, same as if you downed an ME-109, or a German fighter plane, or a bomber, any kind.
They would paint the kill on your-- on your fuselage of your airplane, on the nose of your airplane.
I'll show you some in there.
This, now we're getting into March of 19-- and oh, I was going to say.
In 1944, in winter of '44, they sent-- the Ninth Air Force sent, I think it was two squadrons, which would have been 32 airplanes, to-- to Belgium.
And-- and-- and this was-- the Battle of the Bulge hadn't started, but it was safe, they thought, for us to be that close to where the combat.
We were always 7 to 15 miles away, our fighter bases, from wherever the fight-- if it was static.
If it was fluid, we were further back.
But if it was pretty static, they would gets us close to the troops because we could get there quicker.
But I was going to tell you about this one-- that one battle of-- which would-- ended up being called the Battle of the Bulge, where we were stationed at Liege, Belgium.
And we were evacuated three times in the middle of the night because this is when the Battle of the Bulge started and everything just fell apart.
And in the middle of the night they'd load us all into trucks, and we were-- we couldn't-- we couldn't fly.
The ground-- it was just-- the fog was just zero.
And this is what gave the German such an advantage in the Battle of the Bulge-- wintertime, fog, heavy clouds.
We couldn't fly air-to-ground support.
So we were stuck right there.
But it was really very scary to be awakened in the middle of the night.
And all you can do is-- is get-- get your B4 bag, and-- and you put on some clothes, and grab a pack of cigarettes, and get into a truck, and drive all night.
And they don't even know where they're going to take you.
And-- and sleep.
You-- you know, you're tired, you sleep standing up, for that matter.
That happened to us three times.
Then the weather cleared.
And this was by now 1945, and my squadron was called Blue Fox Squadron.
Our flight leader, name of Harold Snyder, Captain Harold Snyder, we were out on a patrol.
And he saw a convoy in Belgium heading for France-- heading for Germany, trying to get out.
And there were tanks, trucks, men, horses.
I mean, there must have been-- it was about five or six miles long.
So he spotted it, and he called the squadron in.
And we spent a day and a half.
We stopped it the front, which was just a strategy-- just-- and then, we just worked our way from both ends.
And it was just a, turkey shoot they called it.
There was nowhere for them to go.
And they couldn't get away.
I mean, you could run into the woods.
But we were shooting, and dropping bombs, and set the woods on fire for six, seven miles on both sides of it.
And it was a kind of a mountainous, curvy road.
It was really-- I never felt-- like sorry for these guys.
You know, I mean, they were enemy, and I hadn't seen the camps or anything.
But it was our job.
And it wasn't-- it was an abstract thing.
Which, if you talk to pilots, we'll tell you, it-- you're not looking at somebody in the eyes, you know, and killing them.
You're going 450, 500 miles an hour, pushing buttons, dropping the bombs.
And all you see is a blur.
So you don't have to see the mess that you've made, you know.
But on this particular mission, it was such a turkey shoot, as we called it, that I-- I-- we talked about it later.
And some of us felt like, you know, that was too easy.
That was too easy.
There was no flak.
There was no fighting back.
Oh, a little bit, but not-- it wasn't dangerous.
It-- the biggest danger was hitting a tree or something, you know, which happened to a couple of guys.
OK, that was-- that was really-- that was really an experience.
I never forget that one.
I never did see the results of that, but it must have been devastating.
We must have killed thousands of people, and soldiers, and burnt tanks, and half tracks, and trucks.
And it was-- it was just five or six miles of just fire.
And we just kept it going with incendiary bombs, incendiary ammunition.
And then, we went back to Luneville.
And this was about, probably February, March, February maybe.
Or no, earlier than that-- January of '45.
By then, they were going into Germany.
We went down to the Saar Basin, and the name of the town that rings-- there's three towns that ring a bell.
Petite Rochelle, which was in Alsace Lorraine-- Grande Rochelle.
And they're just a little crossroad towns.
And Forbach, France, which is a monument is there-- put there by the French people for the 70th Division, which had been formerly my home unit, who liberated that part of France.
I've been back to reunions there and-- and met with some of the fellows that I served with here in Oregon as a matter of fact.
Beg you pardon?
Forbach?
F-o-r-b-a-c-h.
And it's quite a-- there is quite a-- quite a history around that whole area of tremendous battle that took place.
They brought in some SS, the Germans brought in some SS mountain-- now German, you don't ordinarily fight SS people.
I mean, these were-- they were special troops.
But in this particular battle for-- [INAUDIBLE] Saarland, which I got a citation for-- well, we all did-- was a battle called Nordwind.
And the Germans named the battle.
And it was kind of a mini Battle of the Bulge.
It was winter of '44 and '45.
And-- and it was-- it was a terrible battle, terrible for both sides.
And--
Well, the only-- I was only on the ground-- periodically, the pilots would be sent to the ground troops as forward observers, and-- and be direct-- be controlling the-- the airplanes from the ground.
So we would be with the-- the-- the combat troops, so right out there.
And you had to kind of rotate, you know, everybody had to take a turn at this.
And this was-- this was really not much fun because, you know, you're really-- you're seeing stuff that you didn't have any idea was happening when you were in the air.
And you're seeing prisoners taken.
You're seeing-- in one instance, I saw it was FFI, freedom fighters, a French patrol with assigned-- were assigned to this unit as foreign observers.
And they were patrolling at night.
They captured some Germans, brought them back, questioned them.
And there was a major, an American major that was in charge.
And these French guys cut off the right hands of two of the-- of the Germans and turned them loose, didn't put a tourniquet or anything on.
Just they-- they question them and got what they wanted.
I think there were five altogether.
And they just chopped off the right hands and turned them loose.
And-- and I-- I-- that really got to me.
I went to the major and asked why the hell he didn't try to stop that.
Because-- that's one reason why we never-- if we shot an airplane down, we never went for the pilot in the parachute because the next time it could be us in the parachute.
And they could come and they could tear you to ribbons.
I mean, you're helpless, you know.
So it was kind of a gentleman's agreement between the airmen that you didn't go after another airman.
If you got him, you got him.
And he got away, he got away.

But with the infantry, it was more-- there was more intense feelings, more viciousness, more-- more you got-- you saw your friends get killed, and saw their bodies laying there, and-- and maybe heard them, the last words they said, things like this.

So I could-- even though I asked the major and he as much as told me to shut up and mind me own business, because I wasn't his-- I was just assigned to him as an observer for-- for about-- about a week.
But I reported it when I got back.
I don't know whatever happened to my report, but I said this-- this is the kind of stuff that's going on down there.
And it's not right because it's going to be one-- our turn one of these days if we get captured by that unit that these two men.
How would you like to have them just chop off your right hand and turn you loose with no tourniquet or nothing in the middle of the night.
And so, I felt real-- real angry at that incident.
And anyhow, I'm getting ahead of myself, I think, or behind.
I'm not sure which.
In March we were in Germany.
We were in Germany.
We were at-- we were in Gelnhausen, which is real near Frankfurt.
And Frankfurt on Mainz, which I've been back to several times.
I just love that part of Germany.
Have a special hotel we stay in there, and-- and the own-- I know the owner.
And I mean, we've been there several times.
And he-- he treats us like special, and feeds us wienerschnitzel until it's coming out our ears.
But he was in the Wehrmacht.
Sure.
